mnemonics.title: AVL Rotation Cases
mnemonics.content: LL: LEFT problem needs RIGHT rotation. RR: RIGHT problem needs LEFT rotation. LR: LEFT-RIGHT needs LEFT then RIGHT. RL: RIGHT-LEFT needs RIGHT then LEFT.

mnemonics.title: Red-Black Tree Properties
mnemonics.content: RB-TREE: Root is Black. Children of Red are Black. Equal Black height on all paths (ROOT to leaves). Every node is Red or Black.

mnemonics.title: B-Tree Order Formula
mnemonics.content: MIN CHILDREN = ceil(m/2), MAX CHILDREN = m. MIN KEYS = ceil(m/2) - 1, MAX KEYS = m - 1. Remember: children = keys + 1.

acronyms.usage: Named after the two Russian mathematicians who invented the first self-balancing binary search tree in 1962.
A self-balancing m-way tree data structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, sequential access, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time.
A binary tree where left child < parent < right child, enabling O(log n) average search but O(n) worst case.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a scale or seesaw. Left subtree weight minus right subtree weight must be -1, 0, or +1. If it goes to -2 or +2, the tree is unbalanced and needs rotation.
Imagine grabbing the unbalanced node and pulling it down in the direction of the shorter subtree. The child rises to take its place, becoming the new parent.
Count black nodes (including NIL leaves but excluding start node) along any path from root to leaf. All paths must have equal count.
Think of B-tree as a wide, short building. Each floor (node) can hold multiple apartments (keys). More apartments per floor means fewer floors (height) overall.

analogies.analogy: AVL trees are like professional organizers who keep everything perfectly aligned (stricter balance). Red-Black trees are like casual organizers who allow some flexibility (relaxed balance) but still maintain order.
B-trees are like a book's index. You don't flip through every page; you use the index (tree structure) to jump directly to the right page, minimizing disk accesses.
Rotating a tree is like rebalancing a mobile. When one side gets too heavy (unbalanced), you move the pivot point to restore equilibrium while keeping the order intact.
Balanced trees are like well-organized libraries. Without balance, you might have to search through an entire wall of books; with balance, you quickly find the right shelf and book.
